title: Reductions of Galois representations of small slopes
speaker: Eknath Ghate
abstract: We investigate the shape of the reduction of certain crystalline Galois representations of integral slope 1 and of fractional slopes in (1,2). The proof uses the compatibility between the p-adic and mod p Local Langlands Correspondences with respect to the process of reduction. We give an essentially complete description of the reduction, and show that in the former case it is generically reducible, whereas in the latter case it is generically irreducible.

proof_stages.label: Local mod-p reduction problem and slope parameter
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a primitive modular eigenform f, its mod-p Galois representation restricted to a decomposition group at p, the reducible/irreducible possibilities described by fundamental characters, and the slope v_p(a_p) as the controlling invariant, reducing the nonordinary case to reductions of crystalline representations V_{k,a_p}.

proof_stages.label: Known reduction ranges and the gap to be attacked
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews previous classifications in small weights, large slopes, and slopes between 0 and 1, emphasizing that the next unresolved small-slope range is around slopes 1 and between 1 and 2.

proof_stages.label: New classification theorems for slopes between 1 and 2 and slope 1
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated: for r=k-2 with fixed residue b mod p-1, the semisimple reduction is classified in fractional slopes 1<v_p(a_p)<2 and in the integral slope-one case, with generic irreducibility in the fractional range and generic reducibility at slope one, apart from specified exceptional and p-distinguished cases.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to the automorphic side via local Langlands
proof_stages.summary: Using p-adic local Langlands, its compatibility with reduction, and injectivity of the semisimple mod-p local Langlands correspondence, the desired Galois-side reduction is reduced to computing the mod-p reduction of the corresponding Banach representation on the GL2(Q_p) side.

proof_stages.label: Concrete lattice model for the Banach reduction
proof_stages.summary: The Banach representation is modeled as a quotient of a compact induction by T-a_p, a natural integral lattice Θ is introduced, and its reduction is expressed through a crucial denominator/intersection formula that makes the computation of [4mΘ[0m concrete.

proof_stages.label: Kernel bounds and finite quotient in the slope range 1 to 2
proof_stages.summary: The reduction map from compact induction of V_r to [4mΘ[0m is shown, using Buzzard-Gee observations, to kill the submodule generated by X^{r-1}Y when v_p(a_p)>1 and the θ²-divisible submodule when v_p(a_p)<2, so the computation factors through a much smaller quotient Q.

proof_stages.label: Jordan-Hölder analysis of the quotient Q
proof_stages.summary: The structure of the key GL2(F_p)-module generated by the second-highest monomial is analyzed, giving length and dimension formulas and implying that Q has only one, two, or three Jordan-Hölder factors; the one-factor cases can be read off directly by mod-p local Langlands.

proof_stages.label: Hecke-operator elimination of superfluous constituents
proof_stages.summary: When Q has several constituents, explicitly chosen functions with controlled denominators are acted on by T-a_p so that their reductions generate unwanted Jordan-Hölder factors, forcing those factors to vanish, after which the remaining quotient is identified as principal-series or supersingular via mod-p local Langlands.

examples.name: worked weight r = 2p + 1
examples.note: The speaker fixes the special congruence class b=3 at the first weight beyond Berger’s range to show concretely how a chosen compactly induced function kills one Jordan-Hölder constituent and leaves a supersingular quotient recognizable by mod-p local Langlands.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker fixes the specific weight r=2p+1, computes its congruence class b=3, writes an explicit function f2, applies T-a_p, analyzes the Jordan–Hölder factors and concludes the resulting reduction
